items.question: 1970s pier-style terminal designs at O’Hare and Atlanta’s Hartsfield handled more than how many mainly domestic passengers per year?
items.explanation: Britannica states 1970s pier designs at Chicago O’Hare and Atlanta Hartsfield accommodated over 45 million mostly domestic passengers yearly.

items.question: Airside airport operations are concentrated on which surface next to terminals where aircraft park and are serviced?
incorrectAnswers: The departure lounge gift shop only
items.explanation: Britannica describes the apron, or ramp, as the maneuvering and parking area adjacent to terminals for fueling, catering, and boarding.

items.question: At O’Hare and Atlanta’s Hartsfield, about what share of passengers were transferring without visiting the host city, per Britannica’s terminal-design discussion?
items.explanation: Britannica’s airport passenger-layout section cites O’Hare and Atlanta as examples where roughly two-thirds of passengers connect without leaving the airport.

items.question: Beijing Capital International Airport is cited alongside Atlanta and Heathrow as handling more than how many passengers yearly?
incorrectAnswers: About 200 million in every cited year
items.explanation: Britannica lists Beijing Capital with Atlanta and Heathrow among airports each moving more than 50 million passengers annually in its overview.

items.question: Chicago’s Orchard Field Airport was renamed in 1949 to honor which naval aviator?
items.explanation: Britannica’s Edward O’Hare biography notes Orchard Field near Chicago was renamed O’Hare International Airport in his honor in 1949.

items.question: Compared with a major train station or seaport, what is the defining job of an airport?
items.correctAnswer: Move people and cargo by air with runways for takeoff and landing plus terminals
incorrectAnswers: Sort freight trains into long consists only
Raise and lower ships through canal locks
Store and repair subway cars underground only
items.explanation: Britannica defines an airport as an installation for takeoff and landing of aircraft, typically with paved runways and terminals for passengers and freight.

items.question: Heathrow Airport serves which global city as a major intercontinental hub?
items.explanation: Britannica groups London Heathrow among the very large airports handling tens of millions of passengers yearly.

items.question: Landside airport facilities typically include terminals plus access systems such as roads, parking, and what?
items.explanation: Britannica defines landside facilities as passenger and cargo terminals and access systems including parking, roads, and public transport.

items.question: Memphis International and Hong Kong International have been noted as among the world’s largest handlers of what?
items.explanation: Britannica highlights Memphis (FedEx hub) and Hong Kong International as major global cargo airports.

items.question: Modern large airports provide runways, taxiways, aprons, lighting, and what kind of aids for safe operations?
items.correctAnswer: Radio navigational aids and air traffic control
incorrectAnswers: Only paper maps sold at newsstands
items.explanation: Britannica lists lighting, radio navigational aids, markings, and air traffic control among facilities supporting safe landing and takeoff.

items.question: Which U.S. airport does Britannica identify as the world’s busiest?
items.correctAnswer: Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport in Georgia
items.explanation: Britannica’s airport article states the busiest airport title belongs to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International in Georgia.

items.question: Buenos Aires faces which broad estuary on Argentina’s coast?
items.explanation: Britannica places Buenos Aires on the southern shore of the Río de la Plata estuary.

items.question: Cairo stands near the head of which major African river’s delta?
items.explanation: Britannica situates Cairo just north of the Nile’s transition into its delta on the North African desert edge.

items.question: Dubai is one of the seven emirates of which country?
items.explanation: Britannica describes Dubai as a leading emirate within the United Arab Emirates federation on the Persian Gulf coast.

items.question: Istanbul straddles Europe and Asia across which strait?
items.explanation: Britannica notes Istanbul bridges Asia and Europe where the Bosporus links the Black Sea to the Sea of Marmara.

items.question: Johannesburg is the largest city of which country?
items.explanation: Britannica describes Johannesburg as South Africa’s major industrial and financial metropolis on the Witwatersrand.

items.question: Mexico City lies in which high inland basin that once held several large lakes?
items.correctAnswer: The Valley of Mexico (also called the Mesa Central)
items.explanation: Britannica situates Mexico City in the Valley of Mexico, an extension of the southern Mexican Plateau also known as the Mesa Central.

items.question: Moscow sits on which river in western Russia?
items.explanation: Britannica states Moscow lies on the Moskva River west of the Volga’s headwaters.

items.question: Singapore sits at the southern end of which peninsula?
items.explanation: Britannica locates the city-state of Singapore near the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula.

items.question: Sydney is the capital of which Australian state?
items.explanation: Britannica identifies Sydney as the capital of New South Wales on Australia’s southeastern coast.

items.question: Tokyo is located on which Japanese island?
items.explanation: Britannica places Tokyo on the Pacific coast of eastern Honshu, Japan’s largest island.

items.question: Which river runs through London?
items.explanation: Britannica describes London as stretching along both banks of the Thames in southeastern England.

items.question: Construction of the Taj Mahal’s main mausoleum spanned roughly which years?
items.explanation: UNESCO notes construction from 1632–1648 CE, with mosque, guest house, and gateway works continuing to about 1653.

items.question: Petra was the capital of which ancient Arab kingdom?
items.explanation: Britannica identifies Petra as chief city of the Nabataean kingdom, a trading hub controlling incense routes.

items.question: The Eiffel Tower in Paris was built for which 1889 event?
items.correctAnswer: The Exposition Universelle (world’s fair) celebrating the French Revolution’s centennial
items.explanation: Britannica ties the Eiffel Tower to the 1889 Paris world’s fair marking the centennial of the French Revolution.

items.question: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only ancient wonder that is what today?
incorrectAnswers: Known only from satellite radar with no ruins
items.explanation: Britannica’s Seven Wonders list calls the Pyramids of Giza the only ancient wonder largely surviving today.

items.question: The Statue of Liberty stands on which island in New York Harbor?
items.explanation: Britannica places the Statue of Liberty on Liberty Island in Upper New York Bay.

items.question: The Sydney Opera House’s sail-like shells overlook which harbor?
items.explanation: Britannica situates the Sydney Opera House on Bennelong Point projecting into Sydney Harbour.

items.question: The Taj Mahal in Agra is described as an immense mausoleum built mainly of what material?
items.explanation: UNESCO describes the Taj Mahal as an immense white-marble mausoleum commissioned by Mughal emperor Shah Jahan.

items.question: The Taj Mahal stands on which river’s right bank?
items.explanation: UNESCO places the Taj Mahal on the Yamuna’s right bank within a large Mughal garden in Agra District.

items.question: The ancient city of Petra in Jordan is famous for monuments carved mainly from what rock?
items.explanation: Britannica describes Petra’s rose-red sandstone cliffs hollowed into tombs and temples along the Siq gorge.

items.question: When the Eiffel Tower opened in 1889, about how tall was it—including its flag—making it the tallest structure in the world at the time?
incorrectAnswers: About 50 meters—shorter than a soccer pitch
About 600 meters—twice as tall as today
items.explanation: Britannica records the tower stood about 300 m to the top of the flagpole at opening (later retrofits and antennas changed the exact height over time).

items.question: Which French sculptor designed the Statue of Liberty’s copper skin and overall form?
items.explanation: Britannica credits Bartholdi with the statue’s design; Gustave Eiffel engineered its internal iron framework.

items.question: A modern paved highway linking Pakistan to China’s Xinjiang follows part of which historic corridor?
items.explanation: Britannica notes a surviving paved highway connects Pakistan and Xinjiang along a former Silk Road alignment.

items.question: About how long was the main overland Silk Road caravan track?
items.explanation: Britannica gives the caravan route’s length as about 4,000 miles (over 6,400 km) to the Levant and Anatolia.

items.question: Few travelers covered the entire Silk Road; goods instead moved how?
items.correctAnswer: In stages through a chain of middlemen
incorrectAnswers: Only by single riders in one sprint
items.explanation: Britannica states goods were relayed in staggered legs by intermediaries rather than one caravan end to end.

items.question: Goods from China moved west along the Silk Road mainly over land, then often continued to Europe across what body of water?
incorrectAnswers: The Caspian Sea exclusively with no onward sea leg
items.explanation: Britannica explains merchandise reaching the Levant was shipped across the Mediterranean toward European markets.

items.question: Scholars widely associate the Silk Road with helping spread which 14th-century pandemic path westward?
items.explanation: Britannica states many scholars think plague bacteria reached Europe along Silk Road connections during the mid-14th-century pandemic.

items.question: The Silk Road ancient caravan network linked China with which western cultural sphere?
items.correctAnswer: The Roman world and later medieval European kingdoms
items.explanation: Britannica describes the Silk Road as linking China with Rome and later Europe while moving silk west and goods like wool, gold, and silver east.

items.question: The Silk Road crossed which major central Asian mountain range between China and Afghanistan?
items.explanation: Britannica traces the route climbing the Pamirs after skirting the Takla Makan Desert.

items.question: Which Chinese capital marks the traditional eastern origin of the overland Silk Road?
items.explanation: Britannica places the Silk Road’s start at Chang’an (Xi’an), whence caravans headed west along the Great Wall corridor.

items.question: Which UN regional commission proposed a trans-Asian highway parallel to the old overland route?
items.correctAnswer: The UN Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (UNESCAP)
items.explanation: Britannica mentions UNESCAP’s plan for a trans-Asian highway companion to the historic road.

items.question: Which Venetian traveler famously used the revived Mongol-era Silk Road route to reach Cathay (China)?
incorrectAnswers: Ibn Battuta only for this fact
items.explanation: Britannica records Marco Polo traveling the route under Mongol rule in the 13th–14th centuries.

items.question: Which religion from the Indian subcontinent reached China along Silk Road exchange networks?
items.explanation: Britannica notes Indian merchants and missionaries exposed China to Buddhism via Silk Road connections.

items.question: Antipater of Sidon, associated with early wonder lists, lived in which century bce?
items.explanation: Britannica cites Antipater of Sidon among 2nd-century-bce authors who helped shape the famous seven.

items.question: Classical lists of seven wonders were compiled by observers from which cultural sphere?
items.explanation: Britannica frames the best-known list as achievements of the ancient Mediterranean and Middle East recorded by Greco-Roman writers.

items.question: Later eras compiled additional “seven wonders” lists covering which other categories?
items.correctAnswer: Medieval architecture, modern architecture, natural wonders, and engineering feats
items.explanation: Britannica notes successors listed medieval buildings, natural wonders, U.S. natural sites, modern architecture, and engineering achievements.

items.question: Some early wonder lists substituted the Walls of Babylon or the palace of which Persian king for another entry?
items.explanation: Britannica notes variant lists swapped in Babylon’s walls or Cyrus of Persia’s palace for one of the standard monuments.

items.question: The Colossus of Rhodes was a huge bronze statue erected at the harbour of which Greek island?
items.explanation: Britannica places the Colossus of Rhodes at that island’s harbour to commemorate lifting a siege.

items.question: The Hanging Gardens of Babylon are generally ascribed in tradition to rulers of which ancient city-state empire?
items.correctAnswer: Neo-Babylonian rulers such as Nebuchadrezzar II (among other attributions)
items.explanation: Britannica notes the gardens are ascribed to figures like Nebuchadrezzar II, Sammu-ramat, or Sennacherib depending on tradition.

items.question: The Mausoleum of Halicarnassus was the tomb of which Anatolian ruler?
items.explanation: Britannica describes the Mausoleum as the monumental tomb of King Mausolus built by Artemisia II.

items.question: The Pharos lighthouse of Alexandria stood on which island off the Egyptian coast?
items.explanation: Britannica identifies the Lighthouse of Alexandria with the island of Pharos in the harbour.

items.question: The Statue of Zeus at Olympia was created about 430 bce by which Athenian sculptor?
items.explanation: Britannica attributes the gold-and-ivory Statue of Zeus to Phidias of Athens.

items.question: The Temple of Artemis celebrated in the ancient wonder lists stood near which Anatolian city?
items.explanation: Britannica lists the Temple of Artemis at Ephesus among the canonical Seven Wonders.

items.question: Which ancient wonder is described as the oldest and the only one substantially still existing today?
items.explanation: Britannica’s classical Seven Wonders list names the Pyramids of Giza as oldest and largely still standing.
